body.raw:
# Part 11 — Fault Tolerance & Resilience ✅ COMPLETE

Keeping systems serving despite failures — unified by one idea: **failure is the steady state, so break the fault→failure chain with redundancy and the resilience patterns, make everything idempotent so recovery is safe, degrade instead of collapse, avoid distributed transactions (use sagas), and plan+test disaster recovery — recover fast (MTTR) rather than chase fault-freedom.**

---

## Lessons

| # | Lesson | Core idea |
|---|--------|-----------|
| 11.1 | [Failure Models & Fallacies](11.1-failure-models-fallacies.md) | Fault→error→failure; crash/omission/timing/Byzantine models; fallacies as a checklist; independent vs correlated failures; optimize MTTR |
| 11.2 | [Redundancy, Replication, Failover](11.2-redundancy-replication-failover.md) | No SPOF; active-active vs active-passive; N+ headroom; fenced+quorum-gated+caught-up failover; spread across failure domains |
| 11.3 | [Resilience Patterns](11.3-resilience-patterns.md) | Timeout + retry(backoff/jitter/caps) + circuit breaker(closed/open/half-open) + bulkhead → prevent cascades & metastable failures |
| 11.4 | [Graceful Degradation & Load Shedding](11.4-graceful-degradation-load-shedding.md) | Degrade don't collapse; fallbacks + disable non-critical; load shedding (prioritized); fail-fast vs fail-safe; fail-open/closed |
| 11.5 | [Idempotency, Dedup, Exactly-Once Effects](11.5-idempotency-deduplication-exactly-once.md) | Idempotency makes retries/redelivery/failover safe; keys+dedup / state-based / CAS; at-least-once + idempotency = exactly-once effects |
| 11.6 | [Distributed Transactions: 2PC/3PC](11.6-distributed-transactions-2pc-3pc.md) | 2PC prepare/commit; the blocking problem (coordinator crash → stuck); CP/fragile; 3PC impractical; avoid distributed transactions |
| 11.7 | [Sagas & Compensating Transactions](11.7-sagas-compensating-transactions.md) | Local transactions + compensations; orchestration vs choreography; no isolation; irreversible-last ordering; the microservices answer |
| 11.8 | [Disaster Recovery: RPO/RTO](11.8-disaster-recovery-rpo-rto.md) | RPO (data loss) / RTO (downtime); backups ≠ replicas (3-2-1, PITR, immutable); backup/restore→pilot-light→warm-standby→active-active; TEST it |

---

## The through-line of Part 11

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["11.1 Failure models & fallacies\n(assume everything fails)"] --> B["11.2 Redundancy & failover\n(no SPOF; fenced failover)"]
    B --> C["11.3 Resilience patterns\n(timeout/retry/breaker/bulkhead)"]
    C --> D["11.4 Graceful degradation & shedding\n(degrade don't collapse)"]
    D --> E["11.5 Idempotency\n(makes recovery safe → exactly-once effects)"]
    E --> F["11.6 Distributed transactions (2PC)\n(blocking → avoid)"]
    F --> G["11.7 Sagas\n(cross-service consistency without 2PC)"]
    G --> H["11.8 Disaster recovery\n(RPO/RTO; multi-region; test it)"]
    H --> I["→ Part 12 Microservices · Part 13 Cloud Native · Part 14 SRE"]
    A -.->|builds on 8.1 fallacies/timeouts, 6.7 stampede, 9.4 delivery, 10.2 failover/RPO| C
```

**One sentence:** Assume failure is the steady state and break the fault→failure chain (11.1) — with redundancy and fenced failover (11.2), the timeout/retry/circuit-breaker/bulkhead patterns that prevent cascades and metastable failures (11.3), and graceful degradation + load shedding that degrade rather than collapse (11.4); make everything idempotent so retries/redelivery/failover are safe → exactly-once effects (11.5); avoid 2PC's blocking distributed transactions (11.6) in favor of sagas with compensations for cross-service consistency (11.7); and plan + test disaster recovery with RPO/RTO-matched strategies (11.8) — always optimizing MTTR over chasing fault-freedom.

---

## The key decisions Part 11 equips you to make

- **What failure model / mindset?** Assume crash+omission+timing (not Byzantine); break the fault→failure chain; hunt correlated failures; optimize MTTR; test failure. (11.1)
- **How to eliminate SPOFs?** Redundancy (active-active/passive) with N+ headroom, fenced + quorum-gated + caught-up failover, spread across failure domains. (11.2)
- **How to prevent cascades?** Timeout + retry(backoff/jitter/caps/budget) + circuit breaker + bulkhead (+ idempotency); often via a service mesh. (11.3)
- **How to survive overload/partial failure?** Graceful degradation (fallbacks, disable non-critical) + prioritized load shedding; fail-fast into a safe/degraded state. (11.4)
- **How to make recovery safe?** Idempotency everywhere (keys+dedup / state-based / CAS); at-least-once + idempotency = exactly-once effects. (11.5)
- **Cross-system atomicity?** Avoid 2PC (blocking); keep transactions single-system or use sagas. (11.6/11.7)
- **Cross-service consistency?** Sagas (orchestration for complex, choreography for simple) with idempotent compensations, irreversible-last ordering. (11.7)
- **How to survive disasters?** Define RPO/RTO per data class; real backups (3-2-1/PITR/immutable); matching multi-region strategy; TEST it. (11.8)

---

## Self-check before Part 12

Without notes, can you:
1. Explain fault→error→failure, the failure models, MTBF/MTTR, and independent vs correlated failures?
2. Design redundancy + fenced/quorum-gated/caught-up failover, avoiding split-brain and correlated failures?
3. Apply timeout/retry/circuit-breaker/bulkhead to prevent a cascade and a metastable failure?
4. Design graceful degradation + prioritized load shedding, and choose fail-fast vs fail-safe / fail-open vs fail-closed?
5. Make an operation idempotent (keys/state-based/CAS) and explain exactly-once effects and its end-to-end scope?
6. Explain 2PC, its blocking problem, why 3PC is impractical, and why to avoid distributed transactions?
7. Design a saga (orchestration vs choreography) with idempotent compensations, handling no-isolation and irreversibility?
8. Define RPO/RTO, explain why replication ≠ backup, choose a DR strategy, and explain why you must test DR?

If any are shaky, revisit that lesson's Revision Notes. Part 12 (Microservices) builds directly on sagas/outbox (11.7/9.8), idempotency (11.5), resilience patterns/service mesh (11.3), and database-per-service; Part 13 (Cloud Native) builds on DR/multi-region (11.8), redundancy/autoscaling (11.2); Part 14 (SRE) builds on MTTR, chaos, incident response, and DR drills.

---

*Reference asset for this part: `../../reference/resilience-patterns-cheatsheet.md`.*

Keeping systems serving despite failures — unified by one idea: failure is the steady state, so break the fault→failure chain with redundancy and the resilience patterns, make everything idempotent so recovery is safe, degrade instead of collapse, avoid distributed transactions (use sagas), and plan+test disaster recovery — recover fast (MTTR) rather than chase fault-freedom.

The through-line of Part 11

flowchart LR
    A["11.1 Failure models & fallacies\n(assume everything fails)"] --> B["11.2 Redundancy & failover\n(no SPOF; fenced failover)"]
    B --> C["11.3 Resilience patterns\n(timeout/retry/breaker/bulkhead)"]

    H --> I["→ Part 12 Microservices · Part 13 Cloud Native · Part 14 SRE"]
    A -.->|builds on 8.1 fallacies/timeouts, 6.7 stampede, 9.4 delivery, 10.2 failover/RPO| C

One sentence: Assume failure is the steady state and break the fault→failure chain (11.1) — with redundancy and fenced failover (11.2), the timeout/retry/circuit-breaker/bulkhead patterns that prevent cascades and metastable failures (11.3), and graceful degradation + load shedding that degrade rather than collapse (11.4); make everything idempotent so retries/redelivery/failover are safe → exactly-once effects (11.5); avoid 2PC's blocking distributed transactions (11.6) in favor of sagas with compensations for cross-service consistency (11.7); and plan + test disaster recovery with RPO/RTO-matched strategies (11.8) — always optimizing MTTR over chasing fault-freedom.

The key decisions Part 11 equips you to make

  • What failure model / mindset? Assume crash+omission+timing (not Byzantine); break the fault→failure chain; hunt correlated failures; optimize MTTR; test failure. (11.1)
  • How to eliminate SPOFs? Redundancy (active-active/passive) with N+ headroom, fenced + quorum-gated + caught-up failover, spread across failure domains. (11.2)
  • How to prevent cascades? Timeout + retry(backoff/jitter/caps/budget) + circuit breaker + bulkhead (+ idempotency); often via a service mesh. (11.3)
  • How to survive overload/partial failure? Graceful degradation (fallbacks, disable non-critical) + prioritized load shedding; fail-fast into a safe/degraded state. (11.4)
  • How to make recovery safe? Idempotency everywhere (keys+dedup / state-based / CAS); at-least-once + idempotency = exactly-once effects. (11.5)
  • Cross-system atomicity? Avoid 2PC (blocking); keep transactions single-system or use sagas. (11.6/11.7)
  • Cross-service consistency? Sagas (orchestration for complex, choreography for simple) with idempotent compensations, irreversible-last ordering. (11.7)
  • How to survive disasters? Define RPO/RTO per data class; real backups (3-2-1/PITR/immutable); matching multi-region strategy; TEST it. (11.8)

Self-check before Part 12

Without notes, can you:

  1. Explain fault→error→failure, the failure models, MTBF/MTTR, and independent vs correlated failures?
  2. Design redundancy + fenced/quorum-gated/caught-up failover, avoiding split-brain and correlated failures?
  3. Apply timeout/retry/circuit-breaker/bulkhead to prevent a cascade and a metastable failure?
  4. Design graceful degradation + prioritized load shedding, and choose fail-fast vs fail-safe / fail-open vs fail-closed?
  5. Make an operation idempotent (keys/state-based/CAS) and explain exactly-once effects and its end-to-end scope?
  6. Explain 2PC, its blocking problem, why 3PC is impractical, and why to avoid distributed transactions?
  7. Design a saga (orchestration vs choreography) with idempotent compensations, handling no-isolation and irreversibility?
  8. Define RPO/RTO, explain why replication ≠ backup, choose a DR strategy, and explain why you must test DR?

If any are shaky, revisit that lesson's Revision Notes.
